items.title: Replace Python scan filter with native C# cardiac gating implementation
items.body:
## Summary
This PR replaces the Python-based scan filter pipeline with a native C# implementation using a new `CardiacGatingMRI` library. The change eliminates Python runtime dependencies and provides retrospective cardiac gating directly in the desktop application.

## Key Changes

### ScanFilterService.cs
- **Removed Python dependency**: Eliminated `PythonRuntime` usage and `EnsurePythonInitialized()` method
- **Replaced with C# gating pipeline**: Now uses `MrdReader`, `CardiogramReader`, `GatingEngine`, and `ImageReconstructor` from the `CardiacGatingMRI` namespace
- **Updated method signature**: `RunFilterAsync()` now accepts:
  - `string[] mrdPaths` instead of `string dicomFolder` (supports multiple MRD scan files)
  - `string[]? lineTimesPaths` for optional per-line timing data
  - `int baseScanIndex` and `double sensitivity` parameters for gating control
- **New result metrics**: `ScanFilterResult` now reports:
  - `TotalLines`, `CleanBaseLines`, `ReplacedLines`, `UnfixableLines` (instead of frame/cycle counts)
  - `PngPath` and `DicomPath` for output file locations
- **Added input validation**: Comprehensive checks for CSV, MRD files, and base scan index
- **Added helper method**: `LoadLineTimes()` to parse optional line timing CSV files with fallback to evenly-spaced 8ms intervals

### MainViewModel.cs
- **Updated filter workflow**: Changed from DICOM folder selection to multi-select MRD file dialog
- **Removed Python initialization**: Eliminated `EnsurePythonInitialized()` call
- **Updated result display**: Changed status messages and summary to reflect new gating metrics
- **Added SaveImageCommand**: New command to save current image as DICOM or PNG to patient file
- **Implemented image save methods**:
  - `SaveImageToPatientFile()`: Dialog-driven save with format selection
  - `SaveBitmapSourceAsPng()`: PNG export utility
  - `SaveBitmapSourceAsDicom()`: Creates DICOM Secondary Capture with proper metadata

### MainWindow.xaml
- **Added "Save to Patient File" button**: New UI control with purple styling and tooltip for image export functionality

### Project Configuration
- **Added CardiacGatingMRI project reference**: Links to the new native C# gating library
- **Added SixLabors.ImageSharp NuGet package**: For image processing support (v3.1.7)

## Implementation Details
- The gating engine processes multiple MRD scans against a cardiogram signal to identify and replace motion-corrupted k-space lines
- Line timing can be loaded from external CSV files or auto-generated with 8ms spacing per PE line
- Output includes both PNG preview and DICOM file formats
- All Python interop code has been completely removed, improving reliability and reducing deployment complexity

items.title: feat(dsp): Native C++ Dynamics Helper Implementation
items.body:
This PR implements a narrow C++ native helper for the recursive envelope and gain calculation loop inside the multiband dynamics compressor, dramatically improving render loop performance while keeping Python as the reference specification.

**Key Changes:**
* Extracts `_compute_gain_python` from `_compress_band` to formalize the boundary contract.
* Adds `cpp/include/cognis_dsp/dynamics_executor.hpp` and `cpp/src/dynamics_executor.cpp` containing the `compute_gain` logic.
* Binds the new executor via pybind11 in `cpp/src/pybind_module.cpp`.
* Implements optional availability tracking (`_NATIVE_AVAILABLE`) and explicit fallback in `dynamics.py`.
* Strengthens the `test_dynamics.py` suite to assert mathematical equivalence between native and python paths and explicitly verify the fallback handling logic.
* Refactors the `scripts/benchmark_render_loop.py` script to leverage the new observability pattern and verify the dramatic performance improvements.
* Updates `AGENTS.md` and `README.md` to indicate the expanded scope of the optional native backend helper.

**Verification:**
* `pytest` (all 36 tests passing)
* `validate_native.sh` (compiled cleanly, proven tests, benchmark success)
* Dynamics component rendering time reduced by nearly ~8x under the profile workload.

Adds **C, Go, and Java** as first-class `sugar-high/presets`, introduces optional **`typeKeywords`** so built-in types use the existing `class` token (distinct from `keyword`), expands docs/tests, and trims the homepage carousel so non-web samples live next to the preset examples.

## Core

- **`HighlightOptions.typeKeywords`**: optional `Set<string>`; matched before `keywords`, emitted as the `class` token type (same slot themes already use for type-like highlighting).

## Presets (`sugar-high/presets`)

- New: **`c`**, **`go`**, **`java`** with shared **`clike-base`** comment rules (`//`, `/* */`).
- **`typeKeywords`** for built-in / primitive-style tokens; remaining language words stay in **`keywords`**.

## Docs

- **Carousel**: removed long Python / C / Java cards; keeps web-oriented stack (Rust, CSS, JS literals, TS).
- **Install banner**: preset mapping example, **C / Java / Python** sample blocks with comments and spacing; samples use **`highlight(code, preset)` + Codice `asMarkup`** so highlighting matches sugar-high (Codice’s built-in extension map only wires css/py/rs today).
- **`syntax-highlight-presets`**: adds `c`, `go`, `java` for the live editor extension picker where applicable.

## Tests

- Preset tests for **c**, **go**, **java**; **css** regression; **`typeKeywords`** unit test in `tokenize.test.ts`.

## Remark plugin

- Unchanged behavior: fence language must match an **exported preset name** exactly (e.g. ` ```c ` → `presets.c`).

Add C and C++ grammar support via tree-sitter-cpp. One feature flag (lang-cpp, included in default), one shared handler module (cpp.rs) for both 'c' and 'cpp' language names, seven file extensions registered in the autodetection pipeline.

- `crates/code-analyze-core/src/languages/cpp.rs` -- new handler: ELEMENT_QUERY, CALL_QUERY, REFERENCE_QUERY, IMPORT_QUERY, find_method_for_receiver, extract_inheritance; 6 unit tests

Capture names aligned with all other language handlers (@func_name, @class_name, @call, @type_ref, @import_path).

items.title: Remove YouTube Python sidecar responsibilities and move remaining metrics to native C++
items.body:
This PR completes the YouTube sidecar reduction by moving the remaining runtime responsibilities into native C++ services and cleaning out the now-obsolete Python integration path.

## What changed

### Native YouTube services
- added a native `YouTubeChannelStatsService` to fetch channel subscriber count from the official YouTube API
- added a native `YouTubeLiveStatusService` to poll live/offline state and current viewer count
- wired both services into `YouTubeRuntimeCoordinator`
- ensured YouTube runtime services are stopped cleanly during shutdown

### YouTube platform lifecycle
- kept actively started YouTube features separate from always-on runtime polling
- retained the native `YouTubeLiveChatService` as the active live chat path
- kept the YouTube subscriber event poller as a platform feature rather than folding it into runtime

### Sidecar cleanup
- removed YouTube subscriber-count ownership from the Python sidecar path
- removed YouTube chat scraping/emission from the Python sidecar path
- removed reliance on the YouTube sidecar for live/viewer metrics
- cleaned up stale `PlatformControl` YouTube sidecar code and replaced it with native feature start/stop flow
- removed the remaining obsolete YouTube sidecar file/path from active use

### Build / project fixes
- added the new native YouTube service files to the Visual Studio project
- fixed linker issues caused by the YouTube feature split
- fixed the `AppConfig` forward declaration mismatch warning

## Result

YouTube is now handled natively for:

- subscriber count
- live/offline status
- live viewer count
- live chat
- subscriber event polling

This removes the final practical need for the YouTube Python sidecar and simplifies the YouTube integration model considerably.

## Notes

- `YouTubeLiveChatService` remains started from the active YouTube platform start path for now
- a future cleanup can move that start/stop lifecycle into `YouTubeRuntimeCoordinator` if we decide chat should be owned by the always-on YouTube runtime as well (see #163)

## Fixes

Fixes #162

items.body:
Bumps [cffi](https://github.com/python-cffi/cffi) from 1.15.1 to 2.0.0.
Release notes

Sourced from cffi's releases.

v2.0.0

What's Changed

  • Add Python 3.14 support.
  • Add CPython free-threaded support (3.14t+ only) - huge thanks to the folks at Quansight Labs for all the work to get this one sorted!
  • Drop Python <= 3.8 support.
  • Fix order dependency affecting nested type size calculation (#148).

Full Changelog: https://github.com/python-cffi/cffi/compare/v1.17.1...v2.0.0

v2.0.0b1

What's Changed

  • Add Python 3.14 support.
  • Add CPython free-threaded support (3.14t+ only).
  • Drop Python <= 3.8 support.
  • Fix order dependency affecting nested type size calculation (#148).

Full Changelog: https://github.com/python-cffi/cffi/compare/v1.17.1...v2.0.0b1

v1.17.1

  • Fix failing distutils.msvc9compiler imports under Windows (#118).
  • ffibuilder.emit_python_code() and ffibuiler.emit_c_code() accept file-like objects (#115).
  • ffiplatform calls are bypassed by ffibuilder.emit_python_code() and ffibuilder.emit_c_code() (#81).

Full Changelog: https://github.com/python-cffi/cffi/compare/v1.17.0...v1.17.1

v1.17.0

  • Add support for Python 3.13.
    • Free-threaded CPython builds (i.e. python3.13t and the 3.13t ABI) are not currently supported.
  • In API mode, when you get a function from a C library by writing fn = lib.myfunc, you get an object of a special type for performance reasons, instead of a <cdata 'C-function-type'>. Before version 1.17 you could only call such objects. You could write ffi.addressof(lib, "myfunc") in order to get a real <cdata> object, based on the idea that in these cases in C you'd usually write &myfunc instead of myfunc. In version 1.17, the special object lib.myfunc can now be passed in many places where CFFI expects a regular <cdata> object. For example, you can now pass it as a callback to a C function call, or write it inside a C structure field of the correct pointer-to-function type, or use ffi.cast() or ffi.typeof() on it.

Full Changelog: https://github.com/python-cffi/cffi/compare/v1.16.0...v1.17.0

v1.17.0rc1

  • Add support for Python 3.13.
  • In API mode, when you get a function from a C library by writing fn = lib.myfunc, you get an object of a special type for performance reasons, instead of a <cdata ‘C-function-type’>. Before version 1.17 you could only call such objects. You could write ffi.addressof(lib, “myfunc”) in order to get a real object, based on the idea that in these cases in C you’d usually write &myfunc instead of myfunc. In version 1.17, the special object lib.myfunc can now be passed in many places where CFFI expects a regular object. For example, you can now pass it as a callback to a C function call, or write it inside a C structure field of the correct pointer-to-function type, or use ffi.cast() or ffi.typeof() on it.

items.title: [grove_tb6612fng] Move direction logic from Python to C++ to fix lambda crash
items.body:
# What does this implement/fix?

Fix `TypeError` crash when using a lambda for `speed` with `direction: BACKWARD` in the `grove_tb6612fng.run` action.

The Python codegen negated the speed with `-template_speed` to implement backward direction. When speed is a lambda, `cg.templatable()` returns a `LambdaExpression` object which has no `__neg__` operator, crashing codegen.

**Fix:** Move the direction logic into the C++ `GROVETB6612FNGMotorRunAction::play()` method:
- Add `set_direction(bool)` setter and `forward_` field
- Negate speed in `play()` when direction is backward  
- Python passes direction as a bool instead of negating speed

This also fixes a type mismatch: speed was stored as `uint16_t` but `dc_motor_run()` takes `int16_t`. The cast now happens explicitly in `play()` where the negation is applied.

## Types of changes

- [x] Bugfix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected) — [policy](https://developers.esphome.io/contributing/code/#what-constitutes-a-c-breaking-change)
- [ ] Developer breaking change (an API change that could break external components) — [policy](https://developers.esphome.io/contributing/code/#what-is-considered-public-c-api)
- [ ] Undocumented C++ API change (removal or change of undocumented public methods that lambda users may depend on) — [policy](https://developers.esphome.io/contributing/code/#c-user-expectations)
- [ ] Code quality improvements to existing code or addition of tests
- [ ] Other

**Related issue or feature (if applicable):**

- N/A

**Pull request in [esphome-docs](https://github.com/esphome/esphome-docs) with documentation (if applicable):**

- N/A

## Test Environment

- [x] ESP32
- [x] ESP32 IDF
- [ ] ESP8266
- [ ] RP2040/RP2350
- [ ] BK72xx
- [ ] RTL87xx
- [ ] LN882x
- [ ] nRF52840

## Example entry for `config.yaml`:

```yaml
# This now works — previously crashed codegen
on_press:
  - grove_tb6612fng.run:
      channel: 0
      speed: !lambda "return 200;"
      direction: BACKWARD
```

## Checklist:
  - [x] The code change is tested and works locally.
  - [ ] Tests have been added to verify that the new code works (under `tests/` folder).

If user exposed functionality or configuration variables are added/changed:
  - [ ] Documentation added/updated in [esphome-docs](https://github.com/esphome/esphome-docs).

items.title: Implement optional native C++ FFT FIR execution path
items.body:
Implements an optional native C++ FIR execution path using `pocketfft` and `pybind11`. The implementation focuses on the `FFT` backend, meticulously preserving the Python implementation as the reference standard and maintaining a clean fallback behavior. The native path mirrors the Python contract explicitly, including dtype, contiguous memory layout, and `mode="same"` alignment. Includes associated updates to tests, benchmarks, and documentation while keeping standard Python workflows completely unbroken.

items.title: Rewrite preprocess_corpus in C++ to eliminate full-file memory loads
items.body:
The Python preprocessor called `f.read()` on the entire corpus before splitting lines, consuming ~2× the file size in RAM (file bytes + `splitlines()` list). On a 14 GB corpus this means 28 GB+ just for I/O, before any n-gram counting.

## Changes

- **`preprocess_corpus.cpp`** — C++ rewrite implementing the identical two-pass algorithm:
  - Streams via `std::getline`; one line in RAM at a time regardless of corpus size
  - UTF-16 BOM detection preserved (full-file read only for that rare case)
  - Unicode-aware `isalpha` / `tolower` covering all Vietnamese code points (Latin Extended-A/B, U+1E00–U+1EFF)
  - Same pruning constants (`PRUNE_THRESHOLD=3M`, `PRUNE_KEEP=1M`) using `nth_element` for O(N) threshold selection
  - `~5×` lower per-entry counter memory (`unordered_map<string,uint32_t>` vs Python `Counter`)
  - 4 MB `pubsetbuf` on the output stream to reduce write syscall overhead

- **`Dockerfile`** (train stage) — adds compilation of the C++ source; `g++` is already present in that stage:
  ```dockerfile
  COPY preprocess_corpus.cpp preprocess_corpus.py train_lm.sh ./
  RUN g++ -O2 -std=c++17 -o preprocess_corpus preprocess_corpus.cpp
  ```

- **`train_lm.sh`** — switches the preprocessing call from `python preprocess_corpus.py` to `./preprocess_corpus`; `docker compose run --rm train bash train_lm.sh` continues to work unchanged.

items.title: fix(ops): remove broad python -c auto-accept patterns (#2304)
items.body:
## Plan
- `plans/sessions/2026-04-06_overnight_run_plan.md` § 4.4 Wave 2-3 (Packet W2-3, flex-c)

## Summary
- Remove `Bash(python -c *)` and `Bash(python3 -c *)` from `permissions.allow` in `.claude/settings.json`.
- All other narrowings from #2356 (`python -m pytest *`, `python scripts/*`, `python experiments/*`, `tmux send-keys *`, etc.) remain in place.

## Why
Issue #2304 explicitly flags `python -c "<arbitrary code>"` as the exact concern behind narrowing `Bash(python *)`. PR #2356 narrowed `python *` and `tmux *`, but left `Bash(python -c *)` and `Bash(python3 -c *)` in the allow list — so the hole the issue complained about was still open.

Rule `.claude/rules/80_permission_model.md` already documents:

> Patterns that were intentionally excluded:
> - `python *` (too broad — covers `python -c "<arbitrary>"`)

The settings.json directly contradicted that by still auto-accepting `python -c *`. This PR closes that contradiction.

All legitimate in-repo usage of `python -c` goes through `uv run python -c` (verified via grep across `.claude/hooks/`, `scripts/`, `.claude/skills/`, tests, and docs), and `Bash(uv run *)` remains in the allow list — so lanes and hooks are unaffected.

## Repro / Validation
**Command(s) run:**
- `uv run python -c "import json; json.load(open('.claude/settings.json'))"` → `JSON valid`
- `uv run python -m pytest tests/unit/ --co -q` → `10697 tests collected in 5.11s` (no permission prompt — the smoke requested in the task packet)
- `make lint` → ruff passes, exit 0

**Config (if applicable):** N/A
**Seed (if applicable):** N/A

## Test Criteria
- **Pass condition:** `Bash(python -c *)` and `Bash(python3 -c *)` no longer appear in `.claude/settings.json`, and `uv run python -m pytest` still runs without prompting.
- **Verification command:** `grep -c 'python -c' .claude/settings.json`
- **Expected result:** `0`

## Tests
- [x] `uv run python -m pytest tests/unit/ --co -q` (collection-only smoke — confirms no permission prompt blocks pytest)
- [x] `make lint`
- No new unit tests — this is a permission-list change, not runtime code.

## Expected impact
- Metrics impact: none (config-only).
- Runtime impact: lanes attempting a bare `python -c '...'` (not prefixed with `uv run`) will now see a permission prompt or silent denial. No such usage exists in-repo; all legitimate callers use `uv run python -c`.

## Risk level
- [x] Low (docs/config only; single-line removal)
- [ ] Medium
- [ ] High

items.title: Add VS Code C/C++ IntelliSense config for esp32-firmware
items.body:
### Motivation
- Fix VS Code IntelliSense include-resolution errors (missing `Arduino.h`, `ESPAsyncWebServer.h`, `ArduinoJson.h`, `LittleFS.h`) that occur when `esp32-firmware` is opened as the workspace root and the editor cannot locate PlatformIO headers and compile flags.

### Description
- Add `esp32-firmware/.vscode/settings.json` to point IntelliSense to the firmware-local `compile_commands` and enable error squiggles.
- Add `esp32-firmware/.vscode/c_cpp_properties.json` with a firmware-scoped `compileCommands` path and fallback `includePath` entries for both Windows (`%USERPROFILE%`) and Unix-like (`$HOME`) PlatformIO package locations.
- Define common build macros (`PLATFORMIO`, `ARDUINO`, `ESP32`, etc.) so VS Code can resolve conditional includes without requiring an initial build.

### Testing
- Ran JSON validation with `python -m json.tool esp32-firmware/.vscode/settings.json` and `python -m json.tool esp32-firmware/.vscode/c_cpp_properties.json`, both succeeded.
- Attempted `pio run` for a full build but the `pio` CLI was not available in this environment, so a compile could not be executed here.

items.title: C++ AprilTag odometry node with image-derived heading
items.body:
## Summary

Ports the AprilTag visual-odometry node from Python to C++ for a ~75% CPU reduction on the ARK CM4 companion computer, adds robust heading handling, and introduces support for both NED and FRD frames. Also includes a new interactive teaching page that explains the coordinate transforms involved.

## Why

The Python odometry node was pinning a full CPU core on the CM4 due to the volume of high-rate subscriptions (flight-controller state, TF, and detections) being deserialized through the rclpy executor. The C++ port handles the same message load at 20-25% CPU, freeing two cores worth of headroom for the rest of the stack.

While porting, several longstanding issues with indoor heading were surfaced and addressed. Heading drift — even a few degrees — corrupts the body→NED rotation used to compute drone position from tag offsets, and on a multi-tag corridor it shows up as position jumps on every tag handoff. This PR adds alternate approaches for dealing with it.

## What's in this PR

### New C++ odometry node (\`src/apriltag_odometry.cpp\`)

- Replaces the Python \`apriltag_odometry.py\` for the default runtime, though the Python version is retained as a teaching reference
- Subscribes to \`/apriltag_detections\`, the TF tree, and flight-controller state
- Publishes \`VehicleOdometry\` on \`/fmu/in/vehicle_visual_odometry\`

### Two publishing modes

- **NED mode** (default): computes world-frame position by rotating the body-frame tag offset using a heading estimate derived from the 2D image corners of the tag. Image-based heading avoids the gimbal lock that plagues quaternion-based yaw extraction when the camera points straight down.
- **FRD mode** (\`use_frd:=true\`): publishes the body-frame measurement directly and lets PX4 handle the rotation internally. Removes heading math from the publishing node entirely. Useful when PX4 has a better view of heading than we do.

### Estimator-gated origin lock

- Before locking the origin and publishing corrections, the node waits until the EKF has optical flow and range-sensor fusion active (on hardware) and then holds off an additional \`origin_lock_delay\` seconds. This prevents publishing corrections to an EKF that is still settling after takeoff, which was the root cause of several \`cs_ev_pos: False\` rejection cases on hardware.
- A \`require_ekf_sensors\` parameter lets SITL bypass the flow/range gate for simulation testing.

### Tag switch handling

- When the tracked tag changes, \`reset_counter\` on the published \`VehicleOdometry\` is incremented so PX4 treats the discontinuity as a reference-point reset rather than a position jump.
- Hysteresis added around tag selection: the node sticks with the currently tracked tag for several frames after it briefly drops out of the detection set, instead of switching immediately to another tag in view. This prevents rapid oscillation during fly-through moments where multiple tags flicker.

### New parameters

| Parameter | Default | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| \`use_frd\` | \`false\` | Publish in POSE_FRAME_FRD (body frame) instead of POSE_FRAME_NED |
| \`require_ekf_sensors\` | \`true\` | Require flow+range active before locking origin |
| \`origin_lock_delay\` | \`5.0\` | Seconds to wait after sensors are active before locking origin |
| \`position_variance\` | \`[50, 50, 100]\` | Tuning knob for how much the EKF trusts the vision fix |

### Interactive coordinate transforms walkthrough (\`docs/coordinate_transforms.html\`)

- Self-contained HTML page that teaches how a drone figures out its own position from a downward camera and an AprilTag
- Eight step-by-step slides with an interactive top-down scene (drag sliders to move the drone or introduce heading error)
- Uses the simplified "one tag at the origin" story for clarity — intentionally avoids the full 2-column corridor layout to keep the math accessible for high-school students
- Designed for the DroneBlocks drone competition curriculum; opens in any browser, no build step, no dependencies

### Script/build cleanup

- \`CMakeLists.txt\` install list no longer references removed script names
- Adds tf2 and tf2_ros dependencies required by the new C++ node

## Test plan

- [x] SITL: full pipeline works end-to-end including an autonomous 9-meter corridor flight using \`corridor_navigation.py\` + C++ odometry
- [x] Hardware (Pi + ARK Pi6X Flow CM4): C++ node CPU usage ~20-25% vs ~100% for the Python version
- [x] Hardware: \`cs_ev_pos\` reliably True after takeoff once the estimator-gate is waiting correctly
- [x] Hardware: single-tag hover holds within ±5 cm after the EKF settles
- [ ] Hardware: multi-tag corridor holds position (heading drift still causes drift between tags — known limitation, tracked as next step)

## Known limitations

- On hardware without a magnetometer, indoor heading still drifts over time. NED mode amplifies that drift into multi-tag position jumps; FRD mode sidesteps it by delegating to PX4. Neither is a complete fix for the underlying sensor problem.
- Optical-flow / range-sensor dependence on hardware means the origin lock has to wait for those sensors to be ready on takeoff. In SITL this has to be disabled with \`require_ekf_sensors:=false\` since the simulated FC uses GPS instead of flow.

## Follow-up work

- Adding \`tag_yaw_track.py\` (single-axis visual servoing) is on \`feature/tag-yaw-track-node\`, branched from this one and ready to stack as a follow-up PR.

items.title: fix: use system Python and pin requires-python to pod range
items.body:
## Summary

- Revert Renovate bot's `requires-python` bump from `>=3.14,<3.15` back to `>=3.9,<3.11` (PR #385 broke all pods)
- Add `--python python3` to `uv sync` in install and sp-update — forces uv to use the system Python instead of downloading one
- Add Renovate rule to cap `requires-python` at `<3.11` (Pod 3 is 3.9, Pod 4/5 is 3.10)

## Root cause

Renovate auto-merged #385 which bumped `requires-python` to `>=3.14,<3.15`. This caused `uv sync` to download CPython 3.14.3, which has no pre-built wheels for numpy/scipy/contourpy on aarch64. It then tried to compile from source, which failed because Yocto has no C++ compiler.

## Test plan

- [ ] `sp-update dev` on Pod 4 (@punkmaniac) — should use system Python 3.10, not download 3.14
- [x] Pod 5 — previously verified with `>=3.9,<3.11` + system Python 3.10.4

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

## Summary by CodeRabbit

* **Chores**
  * Expanded Python version compatibility across all modules to support Python 3.9-3.10
  * Updated dependency management configuration with explicit Python version constraints
  * Enhanced environment setup scripts with explicit Python interpreter specification

<!-- end of auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

items.title: bump python from 3.11 to 3.12
items.body:
## Summary
- Bumps minimum Python version from 3.11 to 3.12 across all packages, CI, Dockerfile, and docs
- Fixes slow `install.sh` caused by gevent 25.8+ lacking macOS wheels for Python 3.11 (forced source compilation of C extensions)
- Python 3.12+ has pre-built gevent wheels for all platforms

## Context
`pyinfra >= 3.0` transitively depends on `gevent >= 1.5`. gevent 25.8+ dropped macOS wheels for cp310/cp311, so on macOS + Python 3.11 the installer must compile gevent from source (libev, libuv, greenlet C extensions), which is slow. Python 3.12+ has pre-built wheels for all recent gevent versions.

## Test plan
- [ ] CI passes (all unit/integration/acceptance tests)
- [ ] `uv tool install imbue-mngr` installs quickly without compiling gevent
- [ ] Docker image builds successfully with `python:3.12-slim`

Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
